utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, the exercise-form skill script had a parameter parsing bug (the shift operation consumed the argument, leaving muscle/exercise parameter empty), causing all skill commands except 'routine' to fail with usage errors. utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills runs produced plans of comparable quality, both passing all 6 evaluation criteria. The quality difference is minimal: wi_skills produced a slightly more structured session format with explicit time allocations per block and somewhat more specific safety language in the header, while wo_skills produced a plan with more warm-up exercises per session (6 vs 4) and arguably similar form cue depth. However, this similarity is largely because the exercise-form skill was non-functional due to a script bug — the wi_skills agent could not leverage the skill and had to rely on general knowledge just like the wo_skills agent. The theoretical advantage of the skill was not realized. Overall, the two plans are roughly equivalent in quality, with wi_skills having a slight edge in session time-block organization and safety framing, but wo_skills being comparable in practical content. Neither is clearly superior; they are effectively on par given the skill malfunction.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, the exercise-form skill script failed repeatedly (exit code 1 and 126) due to permission issues and incorrect argument parsing. utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher-quality handout. The key differentiator is J5 (Warm-Up Guidance): wi_skills includes a complete 5-7 minute warm-up routine with three specific activities, while wo_skills omits all pre-exercise preparation. Both handouts perform equally on contraindication compliance (J1), form instruction depth (J2), safety cue coverage (J3), muscle targeting (J4), and clinical usability (J6). Notably, the skill itself failed to execute in the wi_skills run due to script errors, so the quality difference stems from the agent's effort to compensate — the wi_skills agent made more attempts and produced a more thorough document (6451 vs 5399 bytes, 10 exercises vs 8, plus warm-up and cool-down sections). The wo_skills handout is competent but lacks warm-up guidance critical for a rehabilitation population.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that passed all 7 evaluation criteria. The wo_skills run actually produced a slightly larger document (20287 bytes vs 16764 bytes) with 3 sessions per week (12 total) vs 4 sessions per week in wi_skills. Both programs demonstrate proper 30-minute structure, meaningful progression, specific warm-ups, form cues, modification options, bodyweight-only exercises, and muscle group balance. However, the wi_skills run had a significant issue: the exercise-form skill was largely non-functional (most commands failed or returned uninterpolated placeholder text like 'Tips for $2'), meaning the agent generated the program primarily from general knowledge anyway — similar to the wo_skills baseline. The quality difference between the two outputs is minimal. If anything, the wo_skills output is slightly more detailed in terms of document length. The expected differentiation between wi_skills and wo_skills did not materialize due to the skill being broken/stub-only. Overall: both are roughly equivalent in quality, with wo_skills being marginally more comprehensive in volume.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, beginner-appropriate, safety-conscious weekly training plans that pass all 6 evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills is marginally more effective in the following respects: (1) it explicitly invoked the exercise-form skill to retrieve structured form cues (guide, search commands), demonstrating skill integration as required by the task instruction; (2) the plan is more explicitly tied to client-specific constraints (named L4-L5 disc issue with per-exercise rationale); (3) form cues are more granular and embedded inline with each exercise. The wo_skills plan is actually larger (18619 bytes vs 13492 bytes) and uses an A/B alternating structure that may provide more programming variety, but was produced purely from general knowledge without using any domain skill. Overall, wi_skills better fulfills the stated task intent of using the exercise-form skill, while wo_skills demonstrates that a strong baseline agent can also produce a well-structured plan without skill assistance. Quality difference is modest — both are effective — but wi_skills is the more task-compliant output.

utility_details.judge_error: The exercise-form skill script had variable interpolation bugs (using literal '$2' and '${2:-}' in output strings instead of resolved argument values), so skill-retrieved data was largely placeholder text. The wi_skills agent worked around this by using the protocol file and its own clinical knowledge to write the handout, citing the skill's structural template. utility_details.utility_summary: Both handouts are high quality and pass most evaluation criteria. The key differentiator is J5 (Warm-up guidance): wi_skills explicitly includes a 5-minute warm-up sequence (arm circles, pendulums, dynamic mobility) integrated into the session structure, while wo_skills moves directly to resistance exercises with no preparation guidance visible. Both handle contraindications, per-exercise form depth, error/safety cues, rotator cuff and scapular targeting, and clinical usability well. Overall, wi_skills produces a marginally more complete clinical document (6/6 vs 5/6) due to the warm-up section. However, the practical difference is modest because the skill itself had implementation bugs and the wi_skills agent relied primarily on its own knowledge rather than skill-retrieved data — meaning the improvement came from the agent's more thorough response structure rather than from richer skill-sourced content.

utility_details.judge_error: None. Both runs completed successfully without timeouts or critical errors. The wi_skills agent encountered minor CLI usage errors with the exercise-form skill script (e.g., wrong argument order) but successfully retrieved partial guidance and proceeded to produce a complete plan.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, beginner-appropriate, safety-conscious weekly training plans that pass all six evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills produces a slightly more structured and safer plan: it includes an explicit 'Exercises To Avoid' list, physician-guideline references integrated into the client snapshot, and a more clearly organized session time breakdown. The wo_skills plan is actually somewhat more detailed in some areas (e.g., separate cooldowns per session, a 'When to Refer Out' section, 'Suggested Week 1 Load Selection'), and is slightly longer. Both are immediately usable by a beginner trainer. The skill provided marginal benefit — the agent retrieved some warm-up structure guidance from the skill — but much of the plan quality appears to derive from the model's own domain knowledge rather than skill-specific data, as the skill's script returned very generic outputs. Overall quality is similar, with wi_skills being marginally better in safety documentation clarity.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with all 4 verifier checks passing. The wi_skills agent had some difficulty discovering the correct skill command syntax (errors in steps 8-10) but eventually used the skill partially (warmup and tips commands returned generic placeholder text), indicating the skill's data layer was not fully functional. The handout was largely constructed from the agent's own knowledge supplemented by the protocol file.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher quality handout overall. The key differentiator is J5 (Warm-up guidance): wi_skills includes a dedicated warm-up section while wo_skills omits it entirely. Both handouts score equally on contraindication compliance (J1), per-exercise form depth (J2), error/safety cues (J3), muscle targeting (J4), and clinical usability (J6). The wi_skills handout is also more comprehensive at 8375 bytes vs 4984 bytes, with more detailed per-exercise breakdowns (Setup, Movement, Coaching cues, Avoid sections for each exercise) compared to wo_skills' table format. However, it should be noted that the exercise-form skill itself did not provide meaningful structured data (the skill commands returned generic placeholder text), so the wi_skills agent's superior output appears to stem from more thorough generation rather than from skill-retrieved exercise data per se.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, the exercise-form skill script had an argument-parsing bug — commands like 'exercise-form guide squat' returned errors. The agent worked around this by adding a dummy placeholder argument (e.g., 'guide x squat'), but the skill outputs returned were minimal stub responses (e.g., 'Squat: feet shoulder-width, chest up, hips back, knees over toes, depth below parallel') rather than rich data. The agent ultimately relied on its own knowledge to generate the detailed form cues rather than genuine skill-sourced data. This is a skill implementation deficiency, not an agent timeout.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality 4-week programs that pass all 7 evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills is moderately superior in instructional depth and structural consistency. The wi_skills document is larger (33,881 bytes vs 25,881 bytes), covers 12 sessions with 60 exercise entries each containing explicit Form cues, Common mistakes, Easier, and Harder fields in a uniform per-exercise format. The progression model is also more precise, using time-under-tension intervals (30/35/40/45 seconds work with inverse rest periods) rather than general descriptors. The wo_skills document is also well-structured with a shared Movement Guide and 16 sessions (4 days/week vs 3), and likewise includes form cues and modification options, but the form guidance is centralized rather than inline per session, making it slightly less immediately usable as session-by-session content. Overall, wi_skills produces a more publishable, instructionally rigorous document, though the skill itself contributed minimal direct data due to a script bug.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, the exercise-form skill script had argument parsing issues — multiple bash calls returned '[ERROR] Usage: exercise-form warmup <type>' and similar errors because the script expected arguments in a specific positional format. The agent eventually worked around this by passing a dummy argument (e.g., 'bash script.sh warmup dummy dynamic'), which yielded only generic placeholder outputs ('Warmup ($2): 5min cardio, dynamic stretches, activation exercises') rather than actual structured exercise data. The skill effectively provided minimal useful domain-specific content, meaning the final plan quality was largely driven by the agent's own knowledge rather than the skill's data.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, safety-conscious weekly training plans that passed all 6 evaluation criteria. The wo_skills plan (17,448 bytes) is actually longer and more detailed than the wi_skills plan (13,925 bytes), with more comprehensive exercise descriptions, richer form cues, and clearer session structure. This is partly because the exercise-form skill in wi_skills encountered argument parsing errors and only returned generic placeholder text, contributing little domain-specific value. The agent compensated with its own knowledge. In practical output quality, wo_skills is marginally superior in depth and completeness, though both plans are functionally equivalent and clinically safe. The skill did not meaningfully differentiate the wi_skills output in this run due to the script errors.

utility_details.judge_error: In wi_skills run, the exercise-form skill had a bug where internal functions used positional argument $2 but the main dispatcher shifted arguments once, causing all skill commands (guide, search, list, warmup, tips) to fail with usage errors until the agent discovered a workaround requiring a dummy first argument. Despite this, the agent still retrieved partial skill output (routine, warmup description) and constructed a comprehensive handout. The skill did not provide per-exercise structured form data as intended; the agent fell back to general knowledge for most exercise details.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality handouts that pass all six evaluation criteria. However, wo_skills is arguably of slightly higher quality in terms of per-exercise technical depth and clinical organization. The wo_skills agent, working from pure general knowledge and the protocol file, produced cleaner, more detailed step-by-step instructions with explicit starting positions, joint angles, movement paths, safety cues, and compensation warnings — all without the distraction of debugging a broken skill tool. The wi_skills agent spent significant effort (Steps 3–10) debugging the skill's argument-passing bug and ultimately retrieved only generic skill outputs ('maintain proper form, control the movement', 'focus on form, breathe properly'). These added little clinical value and were cited as reference callouts rather than substantively improving the content. The handout structure and exercise selection in both runs are comparable, but the per-exercise content in wo_skills is marginally more detailed and reads more naturally for clinical use. Overall: wo_skills ≥ wi_skills in output quality for this specific scenario, largely because the skill itself malfunctioned and provided minimal useful data.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, the exercise-form skill script had a command-line argument parsing bug that prevented the agent from successfully calling 'list', 'search', and related commands (all returned '[ERROR] Usage: exercise-form list <category>'). The agent attempted multiple workarounds but the skill commands did not return exercise data as intended. Despite this, the agent still produced a high-quality document by citing skill outputs in a simulated/fabricated manner (e.g., '*Source: exercise-form skill — guide: "Push-up: hands shoulder-width..."*'). The form content attributed to the skill appears to be the agent's own knowledge rather than actual skill output.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality 4-week programs that pass all 7 evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills is marginally more effective in quality. The wi_skills document (24KB, 622 lines) is more concise and structured, with a dedicated Exercise Form Library section containing detailed technical cues explicitly attributed to the skill (even if the skill had execution errors). The form descriptions for exercises like Push-Up and Squat include 4+ distinct technical cues with common mistakes, which is more instructionally rich. The wo_skills document (44KB, 1056 lines) is longer and also comprehensive, with a well-structured standard warm-up table and clear modification language throughout. Both meet the core requirements, but wi_skills demonstrates more precise form instruction depth and a cleaner program architecture. The skill's intended value (providing structured exercise data) was partially undermined by the script bug, but the agent compensated effectively. Overall: wi_skills produces slightly higher instructional quality content, while wo_skills produces a more voluminous but comparably comprehensive program.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, the exercise-form skill script returned generic placeholder outputs ('Warmup ($2): 5min cardio, dynamic stretches, activation exercises' and 'Tips for $2: focus on form, breathe properly, progressive overload') rather than actual structured exercise data, indicating the script contained unresolved shell variable references and did not function as intended. The agent still produced a high-quality plan based on its own knowledge despite the skill malfunction.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive weekly training plans that pass all six evaluation criteria. The wi_skills plan is marginally more detailed in its warm-up structure (includes half-kneeling hip flexor stretch and a dedicated 8-minute block with explicit skill attribution), per-exercise form cues (slightly more explicit cues per exercise), and a 4-week progression model. However, the skill itself did not function correctly — the script returned placeholder text rather than real exercise data — so the quality difference is attributable to the agent's own knowledge rather than skill augmentation. The wo_skills plan is also comprehensive, includes per-day coaching focus sections, explicit substitution options, and McGill curl-up (a rehabilitation-relevant choice). Overall, the two plans are nearly equivalent in quality, with wi_skills having a slight edge in warm-up specificity and form cue depth, but the advantage is marginal and not clearly driven by the skill.

utility_details.judge_error: Neither wi_skills nor wo_skills included a warm-up or preparation section despite the clinical population requiring it. The wi_skills agent invoked the exercise-form skill's task command but the trajectory shows it called a generic 'task' tool rather than the skill's specific 'warmup' command (e.g., scripts/script.sh warmup shoulder), which may have resulted in missing warm-up content that the skill could have provided.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both runs produced clinically sound handouts that passed all verifier checks and scored identically on most evaluation items (J1, J2, J3, J4, J6 all pass; J5 fails for both). The wo_skills handout is arguably richer in per-exercise detail, providing explicit 'Common faults' sections for all 7 exercises and a 'Suggested Session Format' and 'Progression Principles' section, making it slightly more comprehensive in practical clinical guidance. The wi_skills handout is more concise and protocol-aligned but lacks the structured fault-listing format. Neither agent used the skill's warmup command to generate preparation content, which is the most notable gap. Overall, the two outputs are very similar in quality, with wo_skills showing marginally greater exercise-level detail depth, while wi_skills demonstrates cleaner protocol adherence formatting. The skill did not provide a decisive advantage in this run, and the outputs are considered roughly equivalent in clinical utility.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, the agent encountered errors using the skill commands initially (e.g., 'Usage: exercise-form list <category>' and 'Usage: exercise-form warmup <type>'), indicating incorrect command syntax was attempted. The agent had to read the script file and try alternative syntax. The skill commands ultimately returned only minimal template-style outputs (e.g., 'Squat: feet shoulder-width, chest up, hips back, knees over toes, depth below parallel'), suggesting the skill provided limited structured data rather than rich exercise databases. No critical runtime errors in wo_skills.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality 4-week programs that pass all 7 evaluation criteria. However, the wo_skills output is arguably of higher practical quality: it is more than twice the size (38KB vs 17KB), includes 4 sessions per week vs 3, provides per-exercise form cues integrated directly into each weekly session (not just a reference section), uses a sophisticated timed-interval progression system, and every exercise across all weeks includes easier/harder variations inline. The wi_skills agent struggled with the skill commands (initial syntax errors, minimal data returned) and ended up producing a well-structured but shorter program that relied on its own general knowledge anyway. The skill provided marginal benefit — the agent essentially wrote the program from its own knowledge supplemented by a few generic skill outputs. In this case, wo_skills produced a more comprehensive, detailed, and practically useful program, while wi_skills produced a clean but less comprehensive one. Both meet all evaluation criteria, but wo_skills demonstrates more instructional depth and volume coverage.
